items.title: WORLD ENVIRONMENT DAY: Salesian Missions highlights projects that address climate action in local communities
items.author: Salesian Missions
items.content:
Countries: World, Guatemala, Peru, Uganda, Zambia
Source: Salesian Missions

Salesian organizations around globe focus on environmentally friendly activities, education.

NEW ROCHELLE, NY (June 5, 2026) Salesian Missions, the U.S. development arm of the Salesians of Don Bosco, joins humanitarian organizations and the international community in celebrating World Environment Day held annually on June 5.

The theme for the day is “A Global Call for Climate Action” and calls for the world to rally around the urgency of climate action. The World Environment Day website noted, “With rising temperatures, collapsing ecosystems and disruptive weather extremes, humanity stands at a tipping point. But we are not powerless. The 2026 theme underscores that climate action is not just about reducing carbon emissions — it is about rethinking the systems that power our economies and repairing our relationship with the climate. By doing so, we can secure a safer, healthier and more just future for all.”

Salesian organizations around the globe are focused on environmentally friendly activities and education. Part of this was driven by Pope Francis’ 2020 Laudato Si’, which underlined the importance of education and training that will help youth foster environmental responsibility. Salesians also launched the Don Bosco Green Alliance, an international collective of youth from Salesian institutions and organizations. Membership is open to all Salesian institutions and organizations worldwide.

“Salesian missionaries are focused on environmental education and activities in schools and oratories to educate youth and protect the environment,” said Father Michael Conway, director of Salesian Missions. “From clean-up days to tree planting to using solar power for buildings and clean water projects, Salesians are ensuring the next generation is empowered to help care for the planet.”

In honor of World Environment Day, Salesian Missions is proud to highlight environmentally friendly initiatives in countries around the globe.

GUATEMALA

At Talita Kumi, located in San Pedro Carchá, Alta Verapaz, Guatemala, boarding students continue to implement environmental protection initiatives. One of their most recent efforts is the production of a new type of organic fertilizer through vermiculture, complementing the Bokashi compost they had already been producing.

The new fertilizer is created using the Californian red worm, a hybrid of species that live in soil and those that thrive in manure and organic matter. Its primary food source includes coffee pulp — an abundant byproduct of the coffee industry in northern Guatemala, where Talita Kumi’s student centers are located.

A Salesian explained, “These worms transform organic waste into humus, a nutrient-rich compost that naturally and sustainably improves soil quality. The process is carried out in wooden boxes covered with black nylon to protect the worms from direct sunlight. Beyond reducing the amount of organic waste sent to landfills, this initiative produces a high-quality fertilizer used for growing vegetables and short-cycle crops on campus, promoting sustainability and efficient use of natural resources.”

These techniques are replicable by students in their communities, most of which are rural and Indigenous Q’eqchi. In doing so, the students help spread ecological farming practices rooted in respect for nature.

PERU

Salesian missionaries have been able to improve the learning environment for youth who are vulnerable and are studying at the Bosconia center in Piura, Peru. The project was made possible thanks to donor funding from Salesian Missions.

Students and staff previously found it difficult to focus in class due to the heat at the school. With the project, two air conditioning units have been added. Salesians also added a covering around the pool to limit the sun exposure for students.

Luis Angel Bregante Andrade, a 22-year-old student, was impacted by the donation. He lives in a challenging economic situation, and the area where he lives has a high poverty rate as well as high rates of crime and drug addiction. He studies at Cetpro Bosconia in the storage operations course and hopes to find a good job after graduation to help support his family.

UGANDA

The Salesian community in Palabek Refugee Settlement, located in Uganda, has a new solar power system thanks to donor funding by Salesian Missions. The funding enabled the successful installation of 60 high-quality solar panels, harnessing renewable energy to meet the community’s electricity needs. This significantly enhances energy access for the community, including religious missionaries and residents.

The upgraded system has contributed to reducing dependence on fuel-based generators, leading to noticeable savings in operational costs. The new system also advances environmental conservation efforts by utilizing renewable solar energy and decreasing the community’s carbon footprint. More than 600 people from the youth center and chapel have been impacted by this donation.

Before the project, chapels, youth centers, nursery schools and the community needed more electricity than the existing system could provide. The old system’s capacity was insufficient to supply continuous electricity where needed. The 12 batteries could only power the area for a few hours after sunset, with some lighting lasting just three hours. To address this, the new system increased the number of batteries to 16, including high-quality lithium batteries known for their long-lasting life and resistance.

The installed system now supports lighting, communication, and basic electrical needs within the residence, enhancing daily living and community activities. The improved energy infrastructure has increased the community’s ability to support various programs and activities, fostering development and well-being.

ZAMBIA

Salesian missionaries at the Don Bosco Community in Mansa, Zambia, recently installed a solar power system thanks to donor funding from Salesian Missions. Salesians were able to install 12 solar panels, lithium batteries, and inverters, allowing the system to provide stable and consistent electricity to the main priest’s house and adjacent volunteer house. Additionally, the new system ensures that nearby water pumps are powered.

Zambia is currently grappling with a serious energy crisis caused primarily by its overdependence on hydropower. This energy source constitutes over 85% of the country’s electricity generation capacity, but generation has dropped significantly thanks to persistent and extreme dry seasons.

A Salesian explained, “We had been using diesel generators to help when the main power supply was out, but that was both costly and unsustainable in the long term given rising fuel prices and environmental concerns. Having an alternative, reliable and clean energy source that supports the essential services carried out by the priests has been a pressing need. The installation of the solar power addresses this while also contributing to Zambia’s national vision of integrating renewable energy into its development agenda.”

###

About Salesian Missions
Salesian Missions is headquartered in New Rochelle, NY, and is part of the Don Bosco Network—a worldwide federation of Salesian NGOs. The mission of the U.S.-based nonprofit Catholic organization is to raise funds for international programs that serve youth and families in poor communities around the globe. The Salesian missionaries are made up of priests, brothers and sisters, as well as laypeople—all dedicated to caring for poor children throughout the world in more than 130 countries and helping young people become self-sufficient by learning a trade that will help them gain employment. To date, more than 3 million youth have received services funded by Salesian Missions. These services and programs are provided to children regardless of race or religion. For more information, go to SalesianMissions.org.

items.creator: ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance
items.title: Philippines: Flash Update: No. 2 – M7.8 Earthquake in Sarangani (9 June 2026)
items.author: ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance
items.content:
Countries: Philippines, Indonesia
Source: ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance

Please refer to the attached file.

OVERVIEW: According to PHIVOLCS the magnitude 7.8 earthquake that occurred in Sarangani, Philippines was tectonic in origin and is attributed to subduction along the Cotabato Trench, a major earthquake-generating structure located off the coast. PHIVOLCS recorded a highest instrumental intensity of VII (PEIS), experienced in General Santos City and intensity VI in Palimbang, Senator Ninoy Aquino, Sultan Kudarat. In Indonesia, the earthquake was also felt and impacted Sangihe, Talaud, and Minahasa in North Sulawesi as reported by BMKG and BNPB.

TSUNAMI: PHIVOLCS reported tsunami observations in six areas with the highest waves recorded at 1.48m in Kiamba, Sarangani. PHIVOLCS lifted the tsunami warning at 1419H UTC+7. BMKG also reported tsunami waves with height at most 0.75m in North Sulawesi. BMKG lifted the tsunami warning at 1015H UTC+7.

IMPACTS & DAMAGES:

PHILIPPINES

  • 37 deaths (ongoing validation)
  • 4 missing persons (ongoing validation)
  • 488 injured persons (ongoing validation)
  • 32.9K families (145.7K individuals) affected
  • 8.7K families (32K individuals) displaced and accommodated in 54 evacuation centres (ECs) and 1,804 persons served outside ECs
  • 2.5K houses damaged (460 totally damaged)
  • 10 road sections and 2 bridges affected
  • 12 cities/municipalities experienced power interruptions, of which 6 have been restored.

INDONESIA

  • 1,160 persons displaced
  • 95 houses damaged
  • 2 churches damaged
  • 2 schools damaged
  • 1 hospital damaged
  • 2 other buildings damaged

HAZARDS & ANTICIPATED RISKS

  • Aftershocks – As of 0600H UTC+7 today, around 1,055 aftershocks were recorded, with highest at M6.7 (PHIVOLCS).
  • Landslides – Mountainous and highly sloped areas are particularly vulnerable to earthquake-induced landslides, rock falls, and tension cracks, which could be further aggravated by heavy rainfall or subsequent aftershocks.
  • Liquefaction – In low-lying, water-saturated, and sandy areas, the intense shaking can cause liquefaction, manifesting as ground fissures, sand boils, and subsidence. This liquefaction-induced subsidence combined with changes in sea level can also make coastal areas and active river channels highly susceptible to seawater inundation.

RESPONSE EFFORTS:

PHILIPPINES

  • The Philippine President convened national and local officials and deployed Cabinet members to affected areas to oversee a whole-of-government response, including ongoing emergency operations, damage assessments, and relief efforts for communities impacted by the magnitude 7.8 earthquake.
  • DSWD is leading relief efforts, prioritising food aid, essential supplies, and the setup of safe evacuation centres.
  • Response efforts, led by the local governments, are prioritising search, rescue, retrieval, and debris clearing operations, while addressing urgent shelter needs such as housing repair kits, construction materials, and temporary shelters.
  • The Civil Aviation Authority of the Philippines (CAAP) has restricted operations at General Santos International Airport to government, military, and humanitarian flights only.
  • The NDRRMOC remains on full RED alert together with their regional offices and local emergency operations centres.

INDONESIA

  • BNPB, BMKG , and BPBD issued evacuation advisories and carried out comprehensive evacuations in districts/cities where tsunami impacts were detected.
  • BNPB coordinated with affected district/city BPBDs to collect data on casualties, affected populations, and infrastructure damage.
  • BNPB identified several challenges, including the remoteness of affected areas, with travel between locations requiring approximately 4–5 hours, and disruptions to communication networks caused by power outages.

The AHA Centre sent a letter of sympathy and offer of assistance to NDRRMC–OCD on 8 June 2026. In its response, NDRRMC expressed appreciation to ASEAN and ASEAN Member States and informed that a comprehensive assessment of the impacts and response requirements is ongoing. Should additional assistance be required, the Government of the Philippines will coordinate with the AHA Centre and formally communicate its needs. The AHA Centre EOC Alert Level remains on ORANGE and continues to coordinate closely with the affected ASEAN Member States, remaining on standby should ASEAN assistance be requested.

DATA SOURCES

ASEAN Disaster Monitoring & Response System (DMRS), ASEAN Disaster Information Network (ADINet); ASEAN Specialised Meteorological Centre (ASMC);

Indonesia: BNPB, BMKG;

Philippines: NDRRMC, PHIVOLCS;

Verified news media agencies

items.creator: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.title: Middle East Situation Lebanon - Flash Update #12 (25 May–07 June 2026)
items.author: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

Overview

• The humanitarian situation in Lebanon worsened amid a renewed escalation of hostilities, leading to heightened protection risks, increased displacement, and growing humanitarian needs.

• Protection monitoring and Protection needs assessments indicate a continued deterioration of the protection environment, driven by repeated displacement, insecurity, and housing instability.

• Vulnerabilities are increasing as more households face homelessness, overcrowding, and unsafe living arrangements.

• Syrian refugees and other non-Lebanese populations remain disproportionately affected, encountering barriers to accessing safe shelter and facing heightened risks of exploitation and protection incidents. Psychosocial distress is also on the rise, particularly among children, with growing reports of fear, anxiety, sleep disturbances, and emotional distress linked to ongoing insecurity and uncertainty.

• The Lebanon Flash Appeal, launched in March, was extended for a further three months (June to August 2026) to enable humanitarian partners to continue rapidly delivering effective humanitarian assistance.

items.creator: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.title: Middle East Situation - UNHCR Revised Emergency Appeal for Lebanon (March to August 2026)
items.author: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

Since 2 March 2026, and despite ceasefire announcements, hostilities have escalated, including through strikes in South Lebanon, Nabatieh, Western Bekaa, and Beirut’s southern suburbs, alongside repeated evacuation warnings affecting numerous towns and villages in the south. Security reporting indicates a high volume of military activity and a sustained risk of further escalation, with civilians, first responders, and civilian infrastructure increasingly affected. Ongoing insecurity and evacuation orders continue to drive nonlinear population movements, as families undertake short return attempts before relocating again due to security concerns.

Around one million people remain displaced from southern Lebanon, the Bekaa, and Beirut’s southern suburbs. Families continue to move in search of safety and shelter, with refugees being among those directly affected by attacks, evacuation orders, and displacement. Humanitarian needs remain acute as continued insecurity constrains access and limits prospects for safe and sustainable return. Shelter capacity remains under pressure, infrastructure damage continues to affect access to basic services, and livelihoods have been disrupted, particularly among vulnerable households already facing economic hardship.

Under the leadership of the Government of Lebanon and the Minister of Social Affairs, and within the existing Lebanon Response Plan (LRP) framework, the Lebanon Flash Appeal - initially launched in March – was extended for a further three months (June to August 2026) to enable partners to rapidly deliver effective humanitarian assistance.
The revised appeal calls for an additional US$ 331.5 million to extend the ongoing multi-sector assistance to 1.4 million vulnerable people in need, totalling needs of US$639.9 million.

Pursuant to the revised Flash Appeal,
UNHCR has correspondingly revised its Emergency Appeal and urgently seeks US$ 94.2 million in flexible funding to ensure continuity of assistance amid persistent displacement and instability. In line with its inclusive response approach, UNHCR plans to assist 733,000 individuals (unique) – Lebanese and non-Lebanese, including refugees – impacted by the hostilities.

items.creator: Eastern Mediterranean Public Health Network
items.title: World: Sustaining HIV Impact in MENA: Community Coalitions and Regional Coordination and Leadership - Policy Brief
items.author: Eastern Mediterranean Public Health Network
items.content:
Country: World
Source: Eastern Mediterranean Public Health Network

Please refer to the attached file.

How can the MENA region sustain progress in the HIV response amid funding constraints, service gaps, and evolving public health challenges?

EMPHNET's latest policy brief, "Sustaining HIV Impact in MENA: Community Coalitions and Regional Coordination and Leadership," highlights the critical role of community-led organizations, regional collaboration, data-driven service delivery, and sustainable financing in strengthening HIV responses across the region.

The brief calls for action to institutionalize community leadership, align services with epidemiological need, strengthen regional learning, and protect equitable access to HIV services to help advance progress toward ending AIDS as a public health threat.

Read the policy brief and explore the recommendations: https://bit.ly/4g8fKyv

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: South Sudan Humanitarian Update as of 7 June 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: South Sudan
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • The humanitarian response in Akobo has significantly scaled up, with over 430 metric tonnes of food delivered, reaching about 60,000 of the 80,000 targeted people (75 per cent).
  • The Humanitarian Coordinator in South Sudan, Ramanathan Balakrishnan, together with the UNHCR Regional Director, Mamadou Dian Baldé, visited Akobo town on 4 June and engaged with the local authorities, humanitarian partners, communities, and the United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS) on the security and humanitarian situation.
  • Humanitarian access across South Sudan remains constrained. On 25 May, five contractors were killed during fighting between armed groups in Morobo County, Central Equatoria State, while working on a bridge construction project supported by a UN agency.
  • Although no Ebola cases have been confirmed in South Sudan, the risk of an outbreak remains high due to porous borders and weak health systems.
  • Flooding has already begun affecting vulnerable communities in Unity State, damaging shelters, disrupting livelihoods, and increasing humanitarian needs.

items.creator: Solidarités International
items.title: Sudan: Assessment Report - West Kordofan State Ghubaysh | 03/06/2026
items.author: Solidarités International
items.content:
Country: Sudan
Source: Solidarités International

Following recent access to Ghubaysh locality in West Kordofan, SI conducted a rapid multi-sectoral assessment between 19 and 25 May 2026 in areas heavily affected by conflict, displacement, and disease outbreaks. The assessment identified extremely severe humanitarian needs and a near-total absence of sustained humanitarian response in the locality. The report highlights:

  • Ongoing AWD/cholera and measles outbreaks amid critically degraded health and WASH services
  • Severe gaps in access to safe water, sanitation, hygiene items, and healthcare
  • Overcrowded displacement conditions and increasing pressure on host communities

items.contentSnippet:
Country: Sudan
Source: Solidarités International
Following recent access to Ghubaysh locality in West Kordofan, SI conducted a rapid multi-sectoral assessment between 19 and 25 May 2026 in areas heavily affected by conflict, displacement, and disease outbreaks. The assessment identified extremely severe humanitarian needs and a near-total absence of sustained humanitarian response in the locality. The report highlights:
Ongoing AWD/cholera and measles outbreaks amid critically degraded health and WASH services
Severe gaps in access to safe water, sanitation, hygiene items, and healthcare
Overcrowded displacement conditions and increasing pressure on host communities
Critical vulnerabilities in Ghubaysh hospital, currently operating at minimal capacity
Major protection, food security, shelter, and nutrition concerns across displacement sites and surrounding villages
The assessment also provides operational recommendations and priority response actions to support immediate humanitarian scale‑up in West Kordofan.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: WFP brings food security to the big screen through moving cinema campaign in Egypt
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Egypt
Source: World Food Programme

CAIRO, Egypt – The United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) is bringing issues of hunger and food security to cinema audiences across screens in Egypt through a new awareness campaign, “مش يومي” (“Not My Day”), screened ahead of movie showings nationwide.

Developed in partnership with the Union of Video & Filmmakers and creative production hub Tayarah, the campaign uses compelling storytelling to highlight the, often overlooked, role of food security in everyday life. The promotional video is currently running in cinemas, reaching audiences before film screenings.

The campaign features prominent Egyptian public figures, including actor Ahmed Magdy, iEvents Founder and Chief Executive Officer Amr Mansi, Bon Appetit Culinary Academy Founder Mona El Banna, award‑winning chef Mostafa Seif, and Gracias Founder chef Peri Saleh. Each appears to narrate their own daily routines, drawing viewers into familiar and relatable moments.

In a revealing twist, audiences discover that these are not their own stories. Instead, the narrators lend their voices to the real‑life experiences of individuals supported by WFP in Egypt, underscoring how access to food shapes daily stability, opportunity and dignity.

“Through ‘مش يومي’, we are engaging new audiences in a conversation about food security in a way never done before, bringing our message to cinema platforms in a format that is both relatable and deeply human,” said Rawad Halabi, Country Director and Representative of WFP in Egypt. “At a time when support is needed most, this campaign strengthens our ability to advocate for the critical role food security plays in building dignity, stability and opportunity for vulnerable communities.”

Through these stories, the campaign illustrates how WFP’s support helps individuals build stable incomes, strengthen resilience and secure reliable access to food. At the same time, it highlights how food security forms an invisible but essential foundation of everyday life.

By blending storytelling with an unexpected narrative approach, the campaign also underscores how fragile this foundation can be without sustained assistance, at a time when funding shortages are placing critical food assistance at risk.

The “مش يومي” promotional video will run across 46 cinema screens until 17 June 2026, bringing the campaign’s message directly to millions of moviegoers across Egypt.

For the campaign teaser and short story cut-downs visit WFP Egypt’s Instagram account: https://www.instagram.com/wfp_egypt/

# # #

The United Nations World Food Programme is the world’s largest humanitarian organization saving lives in emergencies and using food assistance to build a pathway to peace, stability and prosperity for people recovering from conflict, disasters and the impact of climate change.

Follow us on X @WFP_Egypt and on Instagram @WFP_Egypt

items.creator: Save the Children
items.title: Children surviving on leaves and water lilies as conflict drives parts of South Sudan to the brink of famine - Save the Children
items.author: Save the Children
items.content:
Country: South Sudan
Source: Save the Children

JUBA, 9 June 2026 – Families and children in South Sudan’s eastern Jonglei state are surviving on leaves and water lilies as hunger surges towards famine levels in parts of the country following three months of escalating violence, Save the Children said.

Many families in Jonglei state have been left without essential and life-saving services since an escalation in violence in March led to the suspension of aid operations in areas and government orders to aid agencies to evacuate, triggering mass displacement.

Save the Children suspended its humanitarian operations in Akobo East - a refuge for people fleeing violence across Jonglei – and relocated staff due to increased violence.

The withdrawal of services followed a similar suspension of operations in February in Walgak in Jonglei after armed gangs looted and vandalized a Save the Children office, destroyed a healthcare centre, and took three of the organisation's vehicles.

Save the Children staff working in neighbouring counties have reported hearing horrific stories of families going without humanitarian aid while flooding worsens.

In some parts of the state, families and children are surviving on leaves and water lilies collected from swamps and seeds reserved for planting, while mothers walk for hours through floodplains to find anything edible for their children.

Save the Children said thousands of children are no longer attending school, while others are being forced into labour or early marriage as families struggle to survive. In areas where schools remain accessible, some children have stopped attending because they are too weak from hunger.

The extreme coping mechanisms come as the latest analysis by the Integrated Phase Classification (IPC) – the leading global authority on the severity of hunger crises – showed that four counties, including Nyirol and Akobo in Jonglei state, are at risk of famine.

According to the IPC, more than 7.8 million people - or six in 10 people in South Sudan - are facing acute food insecurity. About 2.2 million children under five require treatment for acute malnutrition—an increase of around 90,000 cases since the previous analysis. Nearly 700,000 children are suffering from severe acute malnutrition – the deadliest form of hunger that requires urgent medical attention and specialised treatment.

Health workers have reported children arriving at nutrition centres severely malnourished after surviving for weeks on diluted porridge or a mix of salt and flour.

This deteriorating situation has become evident at a nutrition site at Bor Hospital in Jonglei state’s Bor County, where Save the Children operates three different programmes helping to screen and treat malnourished children and breastfeeding or pregnant mothers. Tabisa Ajer, 31, a health care worker at the hospital said:

“We have over 60 children who are severely malnourished right now. Usually, we have 60 later in the wet season when malnutrition tends to spike but the number is high for this early on. The numbers have spiked due to the season and the worsening hunger crisis, flooding and insecurity. A lot of children coming here have diarrhea and vomiting.

“We are just at the start of the rainy season now, and June to August the hunger situation usually worsens. This year is more dangerous than the other years. Insecurity is impacting food cultivation.”

Chris Nyamandi, Country Director for Save the Children in South Sudan, said:

“Solutions to extreme hunger are political. This situation can be prevented and mitigated, before more children suffer. Next month South Sudan will mark 15 years of independence and greater investment in inclusive peace and social protection is needed to prevent violence amidst an escalating, underfunded humanitarian crisis response.

“In an already hyper-prioritised humanitarian system, international aid cuts continue to disproportionately impact those most vulnerable in one of the world’s most fragile states.”

Save the Children calls on all parties to the conflict to prioritize the protection of civilians, respect international humanitarian law, and ensure safe, sustained, and unhindered humanitarian access to already affected communities.

The aid agency is also calling for greater and more flexible resources needed to respond to the dire needs of the displaced populations in strained communities, with funding flows designed to reach local actors delivering principled aid.

Save the Children has worked in South Sudan since 1991, providing children with access to education, healthcare and nutrition services, and supporting families with food security and livelihoods assistance.

ENDS

*******************************************************************************************************************

For further enquiries please contact:

Delfhin Mugo, Media Manager – Africa, based in NAIROBI

Delfhin.Mugo@savethechildren.org

We have Chris Nyamandi, Country Director for Save the Children in South Sudan, available as a spokesperson.

Our media out of hours (GMT) contact is media@savethechildren.org.uk / +44(0)7831 650409

Please also check our X account @Save_GlobalNews for news alerts, quotes, statements and location Vlogs.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Bangladesh Crisis Response Plan 2026
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

In 2026, IOM Bangladesh will ensure that crisis-affected Rohingya refugees and vulnerable host communities become safer, more resilient, and better able to cope with shocks. Through principled humanitarian assistance, IOM will address urgent needs, uphold dignity, and strengthen protection. Integrated disaster risk reduction, climate adaptation, and environmental management will reduce vulnerabilities and reinforce community and institutional capacities. In line with national priorities, IOM will further support safe, orderly mobility and provide crisis assistance to Bangladeshi migrants, diaspora, and IDPs while ensuring accountability and community participation across all interventions.

This plan outlines IOM's efforts to save lives, protect people on the move, and drive solutions to displacement, including through the Organization's contributions to the inter-agency Joint Response Plan (JRP) for the Rohingya Humanitarian Crisis. In 2026, IOM requires $119,549,857 to support 1,182,800 individuals, including internally displaced persons, refugees, international migrants, stakeholders, and local communities.

For more information: https://crisisresponse.iom.int/response/bangladesh-crisis-response-plan-2026

items.creator: Pacific Community
items.title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 09 June 2026
items.author: Pacific Community
items.content:
Countries: World, Australia, Guam, New Caledonia (France), New Zealand, Samoa, Vanuatu
Source: Pacific Community

Please refer to the attached Map.

Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 02 June 2026:

Dengue

• New Caledonia: As of 03 June 2026, dengue transmission remains ongoing in New Caledonia, with 2,003 cases reported since 01 January 2026 and the most recent case dated 3 June 2026. DENV-1 remains the only circulating serotype. Transmission stays comparatively low in Greater Nouméa, where the Wolbachia programme continues to limit spread, while activity continues outside this area, with the highest incidence among children and adolescents, mainly in school settings. The red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect. – Sources: Alerte DASS – Épidémie de dengue (24 May 2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 29 May 2026 and La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de Nouvelle-Calédonie accessed on 09 June 2026.

• Samoa: As of EpiWeek 22 (25–31 May 2026), dengue transmission continues in Samoa, with 157 new cases reported, representing a 6% decrease compared with the previous week, and three new hospital admissions with no new ICU admissions or deaths. DENV-1 (54) and DENV-2 (46%) continue to co-circulate, with most cases reported from Upolu (92%), followed by Savai‘i (7%) and the Manono and Apolima Islands (1%). Children under 15 years account for the majority of cases (73%). Since 01 January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,482 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,475 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect. – Source: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report in Samoa, Issue No. 59 accessed on 09 June 2026.

Measles

• Australia: Measles activity continues in Australia, with cases linked to imported infections and subsequent local transmission. On 05 June 2026, New South Wales Health issued a public exposure alert after a confirmed case from Queensland visited several locations across the Sydney central business district while infectious, with exposure locations published and updated regularly. A total of 49 measles cases has been confirmed in NSW since 1st January 2026. National surveillance data show that cases continue to occur across several jurisdictions, with New South Wales and Victoria reporting the highest numbers, predominantly among under-vaccinated individuals or those with unknown vaccination status, and authorities are urging the public to ensure they have received two doses of a measles-containing vaccine, particularly before overseas travel. The red alert for measles remains in effect. – Sources: Measles alert for Sydney Airport and Sydney CBD, NSW Health (5 June 2026) and National Communicable Disease Surveillance Dashboard accessed on 09 June 2026.

Pertussis/Whooping cough

• Guam: As of 08 June 2026, pertussis transmission continues in Guam, with 17 cases reported in 2026 to date, including one newly identified laboratory-confirmed case since 26 May 2026. Four cases have been previously or are currently hospitalised, and three cases have an identified epidemiological link. No pertussis-related deaths have been reported. Cases continue to affect a wide age range, with a notable proportion among unvaccinated or partially vaccinated individuals, and DPHSS continues to promote vaccination across all age groups, including through upcoming immunisation clinics, and to encourage early care-seeking for compatible symptoms. The red alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Sources: DPHSS Pertussis Update (8 June 2026) and Epi Reports | Epidemiology and Laboratory Capacity (ELC) Guam – DPHSS accessed on 09 June 2026.

• New Zealand: Pertussis activity in New Zealand continues as part of the ongoing national epidemic, with transmission persisting nationwide. As of the week ending 25 May 2026, 40 cases were reported, a decrease compared with 52 cases in the previous week. Case numbers in the four-week period 2–29 May 2026 were higher than in the previous four weeks, with 183 cases reported compared with 104 cases over the preceding four weeks. Nine cases were hospitalised and no deaths were reported. The cumulative total for 2026 stands at 825 cases as of 25 May 2026, while since the start of the national epidemic in October 2024 a total of 4,583 cases have been notified, including 420 hospitalisations and one death. Notification rates remain highest among children aged 1–4 years and infants under one year The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Sources: Pertussis dashboard and Pertussis Report 2–29 May 2026 accessed on 09 June 2026.

Other Information:

Dengue

• New Zealand: In New Zealand, during EpiWeek 22 (30 May – 5 June 2026), four dengue cases were reported, comprising two confirmed and two under investigation. Both confirmed cases had been to Samoa during the incubation period. Given the known serological cross-reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. – Source: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (22: 30/05/2026–05/06/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 08 June 2026.

• Vanuatu: As of EpiWeek 22, 2026, a total of 10 dengue cases have been reported in Vanuatu, including two new confirmed cases since EpiWeek 20, with no hospitalisations or deaths recorded. Cases remain concentrated in the Pango Area Council (80%), with the remainder reported in Port Vila. 70% of cases are female and the majority (60%) are aged over 26 years. Epidemiological investigations have identified links between several cases, with some individuals reporting recent travel to Fiji within the incubation period. Case numbers remain below the alert threshold, and no outbreak has been declared, although a slight increase has been noted and the surveillance team continues to monitor the situation closely. – Source: Vanuatu Dengue Situation Update 02 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 03 June 2026.

Measles

• Americas: Measles transmission continues across the Region of the Americas. Between EpiWeek 01 and EpiWeek 21 of 2026 (ending 30 May 2026), 21,431 confirmed cases and 31 deaths were reported from 17 countries and territories, a 234% increase compared with the same period in 2025. Mexico (11,184), Guatemala (6,655), the United States (1,983) and Canada (1,042) accounted for the majority (97%) of confirmed cases. During EpiWeek 19 and 20, 1,040 new confirmed cases were reported, a 16.2% decrease on the previous two-week period, with declines driven mainly by the United States, Canada and Mexico, while Peru continued to increase, concentrated in Puno. The outbreak continues to disproportionately affect young children, with the highest incidence among infants under one year, and approximately 85% of confirmed cases are unvaccinated or of unknown vaccination status. – Source: Situation Report No. 4 – Measles in the Region of the Americas, PAHO/WHO (4 June 2026) accessed on 09 June 2026.

items.title: oPt: WFP Palestine Emergency Response External Situation Report #90, 9 June 2026 (Reporting period: 1-31 May 2026)
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • Humanitarian and commercial cargo flows increased slightly in May, easing some market access constraints and supporting limited reliance on markets. However, food insecurity remained severe, with high and volatile prices and continued challenges in accessing adequate and diverse food, underscoring the need for sustained cargo inflows into Gaza.
  • At the same time, the closure of Zikim crossing on 24 May left Kerem Shalom as the only cargo entry point, while ongoing restrictions and supply chain constraints continued to limit the availability of essential goods.
  • On 14 May, WFP was targeted by a cyber-attack on its Palestine self-registration application (SRA); the platform was immediately shut down, the intrusion contained, and security measures strengthened. Assistance delivery to existing beneficiaries continued uninterrupted. No other WFP systems were affected.

items.creator: Data Friendly Space
items.title: Occupied Palestinian Territories (oPt) Crisis Situation Analysis (Period: 25/05/26 - 31/05/26)
items.author: Data Friendly Space
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Data Friendly Space

Please refer to the attached file.

The Occupied Palestinian Territory is facing a severe humanitarian crisis, with both Gaza and the West Bank experiencing unprecedented levels of violence and deprivation. In Gaza, following the collapse of a brief pause in hostilities in March 2025, Israeli forces resumed intensive bombardment while maintaining a complete blockade that has now entered its eighth week, preventing all humanitarian aid and commercial supplies from entering. This has left Gaza's 2.2 million residents facing starvation as food stocks deplete, bakeries cease operations due to lack of flour and fuel, and nearly 90% of the population remains displaced with critical infrastructure almost entirely destroyed. A new U.S.-backed peace plan led to a pause in hostilities agreement coming into effect on 10 October 2025, raising cautious hopes for increased humanitarian access and a potential reduction in hostilities after months of relentless violence. However, despite the pause in hostilities, sporadic violations, limited aid entry, and continued displacement highlight the fragility of the pause in hostilities and the deep humanitarian strain that persists across Gaza. Meanwhile, the West Bank has seen a dramatic escalation in military operations, particularly since the launch of "Operation Iron Wall" in January 2025, which has resulted in mass displacement of Palestinians and widespread destruction across refugee camps. Israeli forces have displaced approximately 40,000 people, with officials stating that residents will not be allowed to return for at least a year, while expanded checkpoints and military presence severely restrict movement between Palestinian cities. International organisations and UN bodies have expressed increasing concern about systematic violations of international humanitarian law in the West Bank, including accelerating settlement expansion, land reclassification and forced displacement that multiple observers warn are driving de facto annexation dynamics. These concerns have intensified following the International Court of Justice's 2024 advisory opinion on the illegality of the prolonged occupation and the Israeli authorities' security cabinet decisions of 8 February 2026 expanding civil and administrative control over West Bank land and governance.

items.creator: International Food Policy Research Institute
items.title: Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report – May Thet Htar, Bart Minten, and Ian Masias (April 2026)
items.author: International Food Policy Research Institute
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: International Food Policy Research Institute

Please refer to the attached file.

Summary:

The latest Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report, published by the International Food Policy Research Institute, finds that food prices in Myanmar remained volatile in April 2026, with notable increases in rice prices and continued pressure on animal-source food prices.

Rice prices rose sharply compared to March, signaling a continued recovery after earlier declines, though significant regional price differences persist. Export crop prices were generally stable, while maize prices increased on expectations of renewed border trade with Thailand. Among vegetables, onion, chili, and garlic prices edged higher, but potato prices fell due to increased imports and weak demand.

Animal-sourced foods remained significantly more expensive than a year ago, driven by supply constraints, disease outbreaks, and export demand, although month-to-month changes were modest. Looking ahead, disruptions linked to the conflict in Iran and partial closure of the Strait of Hormuz pose risks to Myanmar’s agrifood system through potential increases in fuel and fertilizer costs, which could affect transport, production, and the upcoming monsoon planting season.

Download the report

Citation: Htar, May Thet; Minten, Bart; and Masias, Ian. 2026. Myanmar monthly food price report – April 2026. Myanmar Monthly Food Price Report April 2026. Washington, DC: International Food Policy Research Institute. https://hdl.handle.net/10568/183116

items.creator: Caritas
items.title: oPt: Caritas Jerusalem: Gaza’s water crisis has reached a critical point
items.author: Caritas
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Caritas

“Water, water, everywhere, nor any drop to drink.” The haunting words of the English poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge feel less like poetry and more like testimony in Gaza today.

Gaza lies along the Mediterranean coast, yet safe water has become painfully scarce.

Years of repeated damage to water and sanitation systems have left wells, pipelines, sewage networks, and desalination plants destroyed or barely functioning.

What once sustained daily life has, in many places, become unreliable or unsafe.

Across neighbourhoods and displacement sites, children walk long distances carrying heavy, empty containers. Parents try to stretch whatever water they can find across drinking, cooking, and washing, often knowing it may not be safe.

The quiet calculation of survival, how much water can be spared today, has become part of everyday life.

Behind this struggle is a system under collapse. Damage to sewage infrastructure and lack of treatment have contributed to contamination of Gaza’s coastal aquifer, the main source of freshwater.

Untreated wastewater, saltwater intrusion, and debris have further reduced water quality. What remains is often unsafe, but there are few alternatives left.

The health consequences are already visible. Humanitarian agencies including our doctors on the ground have reported rising cases of acute watery diarrhoea and hepatitis A, especially among displaced families living in crowded shelters with limited sanitation.

For many children, illness is no longer an exception but a recurring part of life shaped by unsafe water and weakened conditions.

The land itself carries its own quiet loss. Large areas of farmland, olive groves, and vegetation have been damaged or destroyed.

Soil that once absorbed rain and supported crops is now degraded and compacted, reducing both food production and the natural renewal of groundwater. The result is a deepening cycle of scarcity: less water, less food, less recovery.

In response to the breakdown of water and sanitation services, Caritas Jerusalem is providing trucked water and emergency WASH kits to communities with limited access to safe drinking water in Gaza.

The aim is to reduce the spread of disease and help families maintain the most basic conditions of hygiene and dignity.

Still, behind every technical term and every report is something simpler and heavier: families waiting in line for water that may not come, children falling ill from what they must drink, and parents trying to hold together a sense of normal life in conditions that no longer feel normal at all.

Gaza’s water crisis is not only about infrastructure failure. It is about the slow erosion of something far more fragile: the feeling that tomorrow might be easier than today.

By Harout Bedrossian, Head of Resource Development at Caritas Jerusalem

The post Caritas Jerusalem: Gaza’s water crisis has reached a critical point appeared first on Caritas.

items.creator: Ground Truth Solutions
items.title: “Cash provides me with a sense of freedom” User journeys of cash recipients in Sudan
items.author: Ground Truth Solutions
items.content:
Country: Sudan
Source: Ground Truth Solutions

Please refer to the attached file.

Introduction

Since the outbreak of fighting between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces in April 2023, the country has faced one of the most severe humanitarian crises in the world. In 2026, 33.3 million people – over two thirds of the population – require humanitarian assistance, while the conflict has triggered the largest displacement crisis globally, with over 11 million people internally displaced and millions more fleeing to neighbouring countries.

The conflict has severely disrupted the economy, including financial infrastructure and market systems. This has resulted in high inflation, currency depreciation, liquidity shortages, fragmentation of the financial system, and the collapse of formal banking in nearly half of the country. Despite these constraints, markets at the state level remain largely functional, and cash and voucher assistance (CVA) continues to be both effective and strongly preferred. Over 90 per cent of affected households identify cash as the most dignified and responsive form of support.

Local responders, including mutual aid groups, act as the primary frontline providers of humanitarian assistance. Through decentralized networks and community initiatives, they deliver assistance quickly where formal systems have collapsed. These initiatives are grounded in Sudan’s tradition of mutual aid, reflecting a culture of solidarity and self-reliance. Despite being underfunded and operating under significant risk, they have become a critical lifeline for civilians during the crisis.

Complementing local efforts, the Collective Sudan (CCS), a platform bringing together 19 partner organisations, delivers coordinated cash and voucher assistance to crisis-affected populations across Sudan, supporting vulnerable households to meet their basic needs. This includes providing cash to individuals and group cash transfers to mutual aid groups.

To support CCS' mission to identify more effective ways of assisting those in need, Ground Truth Solutions (GTS) collaborated with DataQ to conduct a qualitative study exploring community perceptions of cash assistance in Khartoum and North Darfur. The study sought to understand the experiences of recipients of different forms of CVA, namely cash in hand, vouchers, bank transfers, and group cash transfers, and to identify opportunities and barriers for improving the delivery of assistance.

Adopting a “user journey” approach, the research visualises the full recipient experience – from the moment assistance is needed, through the registration and selection processes, to receiving and using the cash. Through qualitative interviews conducted with 22 cash recipients in Khartoum and North Darfur in December 2025, the study developed four user journeys: detailed personal accounts that highlight key pain points, and recommendations for improving the assistance.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Mali : SOP sur l'orientation inter-agence des plaintes d'EAS
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Mali
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

PROCÉDURES OPÉRATIONNELLES STANDARD SUR LE SIGNALEMENT, ORIENTATION ET RETOUR D’INFORMATION INTER-AGENCE SUR L’EXPLOITATION ET LES ABUS SEXUELS

Endossées le 19 novembre 2025 par le UNCT/HCT

Ces procédures opérationnelles standard (SOP) sont conçues pour guider et définir les étapes et les mesures à prendre lorsqu'il y a une allégation d’exploitation et des abus sexuels commis par des acteurs humanitaires et de développement au Mali. Le harcèlement sexuel entre les membres du personnel n’est pas inclus dans le champ d'application de ces SOP.

L’objectif est donc de faciliter une approche cohérente pour toutes les plaintes d'EAS et de s’assurer qu'une plainte d'EAS reçue à travers n’importe quel canal, est transmise à toute entité concernée pour un soutien approprié aux victimes/survivants, un suivi et une enquête en application de l’approche centrée sur la victime.

L’outil est donc adopté pour assurer la cohérence en ce qui concerne la réception des plaintes et leur transmission aux différentes organisations pour enquête rapide. Elles se conforment à la note d’orientation du Comité Permanent Inter-organisations (IASC) sur les Procédures relatives à l’orientation inter-organisations de septembre 2023.

Les présentes SOP s’appliquent donc à tous les membres de l’Équipe Pays des Nations Unies et humanitaire, leurs partenaires et aux membres du réseau PEAS. Elles ne remplacent ni n'annulent en aucun cas les politiques, règles ou procédures internes des organisations. Elles décrivent plutôt les procédures qui découlent des politiques internes et les complètent lorsque les entités s'engagent dans une coopération inter-agences pour l’orientation des cas d’EAS.

Il est primordial de noter que pour que l'orientation inter-agence des plaintes fonctionne correctement, ces SOP doivent être complétées par l’existence de systèmes de référence GBV/CP à jours ainsi qu’une sensibilisation du personnel et des communautés sur ces systèmes. En outre, des canaux diversifiés, confidentiels, accessibles et adaptés doivent être disponibles pour faciliter la dénonciation de l’EAS. Ceci signifie également le renforcement les capacités des gestionnaires de ces canaux à la réception des plaintes sensibles.

items.creator: ACAPS
items.title: Lebanon: Escalation of conflict and forced displacement in southern areas
items.author: ACAPS
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: ACAPS

Please refer to the attached file.

CONTEXT OVERVIEW

• Since the collapse of the November 2024 ceasefire in early March 2026, hostilities between Israel and Hezbollah have escalated significantly, with repeated Israeli airstrikes, ground incursions, and displacement orders across southern Lebanon and Beirut’s southern suburbs (AJ 03/03/2026; Reuters 18/03/2026; Mercy Corps 05/06/2026). Active hostilities continue, with military operations expanding geographically.

• On 7 June, Israeli forces struck Dahiyeh, Beirut’s southern suburbs, for the first time since the latest US-backed ceasefire plan was announced on 4 June, killing at least two people and wounding 11 others in a densely populated residential area (AJ 07/06/2026; CS Monitor 07/06/2026). The strike was carried out despite a US request not to attack the capital and Iran’s warnings that any strike on Beirut would risk renewed full-scale war. This signals a further geographic expansion or re-escalation of military operations and a potential collapse of the fragile diplomatic process (Reuters 07/06/2026; GMA News 08/06/2026).

• Hezbollah’s rejection of the ceasefire arrangements reached through direct negotiations between the Lebanese Government and Israel, coupled with Israel’s reluctance to link the Lebanon file to broader negotiations with Iran, reduces the likelihood of a durable ceasefire in the near term and increases the risk of continued hostilities in southern Lebanon. The events of 7 June underscore that this linkage is not merely a negotiating posture, but an operational reality: Iran’s launch of ballistic missiles at Israel in direct response to the Dahiyeh strikes, the first such bombardment since the April ceasefire, demonstrates that military operations in Lebanon trigger wider regional escalation, and that any durable resolution of the Lebanon conflict cannot be decoupled from the broader Iran–Israel negotiations (AJ 07/06/2026; NPR 07/06/2026). As such, progress in USIran negotiations remains a critical variable, with the potential to increase pressure on both parties and create greater incentives for a sustained ceasefire — though the 7 June escalation has significantly narrowed that diplomatic window (The New Arab 27/05/2026; Arab News 07/06/2026).

• Since 2 March, an estimated 620,000 women and girls have been displaced, with 85% living outside formal shelters in overcrowded apartments and informal arrangements in Beirut and Mount Lebanon, creating serious protection risks including exploitation, abuse, and genderbased violence. Disrupted protection and justice services have made it harder for women and other at-risk populations to report violations or seek support (UN Women 27/03/2026).

• At least 245 children have been killed and 957 injured in Lebanon between 2 March and 4 June, while an estimated 770,000 children are experiencing heightened distress from repeated exposure to violence, loss, and displacement, with caregivers reporting symptoms of traumatic stress and grief including extreme fear, nightmares, sleeplessness, and feelings of hopelessness (UNICEF 13/05/2026; WHO 07/06/2026).
